question_title: Will the UN General Assembly adopt a resolution condemning the US operation in Venezuela before February 1, 2026?
summary: The question asks whether the UNGA will adopt a resolution condemning/deploring the US operation in Venezuela before February 1, 2026. The US captured President Maduro on Jan 3, triggering broad international condemnation including from US allies. Historical precedents strongly favor YES: Panama (1989) saw a UNGA resolution in 9 days, Grenada (1983) within ~2 weeks. However, Iraq (2003) had no UNGA condemnation despite massive opposition. The Venezuela situation is more analogous to Panama (unilateral action, leader captured on drug charges). The key uncertainty is that 8 days have passed without visible evidence of a draft UNGA resolution or emergency special session being called. While 20 days remain (sufficient time), the absence of visible momentum is a moderate negative signal. Balancing strong historical precedent against current absence of action and "Nothing Ever Happens" skepticism, I estimate 45% probability.

factors.description: Historical precedent: Both closest parallels (Panama 1989, Grenada 1983) resulted in UNGA condemnation resolutions within 2 weeks of unilateral US military action
Breadth of condemnation: Even close US allies (France) and the UN Secretary General explicitly condemned the action and said it violated the UN Charter, suggesting strong support for a resolution
8 days have elapsed since the operation with no visible evidence of a draft UNGA resolution or emergency special session being called - in the Panama case, the UNGA voted on day 9
Counter-precedent: Iraq 2003 saw no UNGA condemnation despite massive global opposition, though circumstances were significantly different (coalition involved, complex UN history)
US under Trump may apply aggressive diplomatic pressure (threatening UN funding cuts, bilateral consequences) to prevent or delay a UNGA resolution
20 days remain until deadline - sufficient time for the UNGA process to complete even if it hasn't visibly started
Maduro was widely viewed as an illegitimate, repressive leader - this may reduce urgency among some countries to defend his sovereignty, potentially weakening resolution support
